environment: A local single-node cluster using minikube or Docker Desktop Kubernetes, with kubectl already connected to the cluster. Before starting each task, run 'kubectl get pods' to confirm there are no leftover Pods from a previous exercise.

tasks.title: Create an nginx Pod and Inspect Its Status
tasks.prompt:
Use kubectl run to create a Pod named 'my-web' using the nginx image. After creating it, do the following three things.
1. Read the READY, STATUS, RESTARTS, and AGE columns from the Pod list.
2. Add the '-o wide' flag to see the node name and IP address where the Pod was scheduled.
3. When you are done, delete the Pod and confirm it no longer appears in the list.
tasks.starter:
# Verify cluster connection
kubectl cluster-info

# Fill in the blanks and run each command
# (1) Create the Pod
kubectl run ___ --image=___

# (2) List Pods
kubectl get pods

# (3) Wide output to see node/IP
kubectl get pods ___

# (4) Delete the Pod
kubectl delete pod ___

# (5) Confirm deletion
kubectl get pods
tasks.solution:
# (1) Create the Pod
kubectl run my-web --image=nginx
# Expected output: pod/my-web created

# (2) List Pods
kubectl get pods
# Expected output:
# NAME     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# my-web   1/1     Running   0          8s
# Note: if it shows ContainerCreating or Pending, wait a few seconds and re-run

# (3) Wide output to see node/IP
kubectl get pods -o wide
# Expected output:
# NAME     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP           NODE       NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES
# my-web   1/1     Running   0          30s   10.244.0.4   minikube   <none>           <none>

# (4) Delete the Pod
kubectl delete pod my-web
# Expected output: pod "my-web" deleted

# (5) Confirm deletion
kubectl get pods
# Expected output: No resources found in default namespace.
tasks.explanation: 'kubectl run <name> --image=<image>' is an imperative command that creates a Pod quickly without writing a YAML file. The '-o wide' flag appends the node name and Pod IP to the default columns, letting you see placement at a glance. A Pod created with 'kubectl run' has no backing controller, so deleting it makes it disappear permanently - it is not recreated. This is the key difference from Deployments, covered in the next lecture.
hints: The '1/1' in the READY column means 'ready containers / total containers'. A multi-container Pod would show '2/2'.
If STATUS is stuck at 'ContainerCreating', the image pull is still in progress. Run 'kubectl describe pod my-web' and look at the Events section to track the Pulling → Pulled → Started sequence.
Instead of '-o wide', try '-o yaml' to see the full Pod spec in YAML format. Glancing at the spec.containers section before the next task will help you get familiar with the structure.

tasks.title: Define, Create, and Delete a Pod Using a YAML File
tasks.prompt:
Save the starter YAML below as 'simple-pod.yaml', then create the Pod with kubectl apply -f. After creation, perform the following steps in order.
1. Run 'kubectl describe pod simple-pod' and check the IP, the assigned node, and the Events section.
2. Run 'kubectl logs simple-pod' to print the initial nginx logs.
3. Run 'kubectl exec -it simple-pod -- /bin/bash' to open a shell inside the container, run 'ls /usr/share/nginx/html', then exit.
4. Delete the Pod using the YAML file ('kubectl delete -f simple-pod.yaml').
tasks.starter:
# Contents of simple-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: simple-pod
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  containers:
    - name: web-container
      image: nginx
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
tasks.solution:
# 1. Apply the YAML file
kubectl apply -f simple-pod.yaml
# Expected output: pod/simple-pod created

# 2. Inspect with describe
kubectl describe pod simple-pod
# Key fields to note:
#   IP: 10.244.0.x (varies by cluster)
#   Node: minikube
#   Last three Events:
#     Normal  Scheduled  ...  Successfully assigned default/simple-pod to minikube
#     Normal  Pulled     ...  Container image "nginx" already present on machine
#     Normal  Started    ...  Started container web-container

# 3. View logs
kubectl logs simple-pod
# If no HTTP requests have been made yet, only the nginx startup message is shown

# 4. Open a shell inside the container
kubectl exec -it simple-pod -- /bin/bash
# Once the prompt changes, run the internal command
ls /usr/share/nginx/html
# Expected output: 50x.html  index.html
exit

# 5. Delete using the YAML file
kubectl delete -f simple-pod.yaml
# Expected output: pod "simple-pod" deleted
tasks.explanation: The YAML approach lets you manage all configuration in a file, making it suitable for version control (git) and the standard for production use - unlike 'kubectl run'. 'kubectl apply -f' applies a file declaratively and can also be used to apply updates to the same resource. The Events section in 'describe' records the Scheduled → Pulled → Started sequence and is the first place to look when diagnosing problems. 'exec -it' opens an interactive shell inside a running container, which is useful for inspecting file structures, checking environment variables, and general debugging. 'delete -f' pairs with 'apply -f' to remove resources using the same file, so you do not need to remember resource names separately.
hints: YAML indentation must use spaces, not tabs. Using a tab character causes the error 'error: yaml: line N: found character that cannot start any token'.

In 'kubectl exec -it simple-pod -- /bin/bash', the '--' separates kubectl's own flags from the command passed to the container. If the image does not include /bin/bash (e.g., lightweight images like busybox), use '/bin/sh' instead.
If the logs appear empty, run 'curl localhost' from inside the container via 'kubectl exec', then run 'kubectl logs simple-pod' again - you will see an access log entry.

tasks.title: Create a Broken Pod with a Bad Image and Diagnose the Cause with describe
tasks.prompt: Intentionally create a Pod named 'broken-pod' using a non-existent image 'nginx:doesnotexist999'. Immediately after creation, run 'kubectl get pods' a few times to observe how the STATUS changes. Once STATUS shows 'ErrImagePull' or 'ImagePullBackOff', run 'kubectl describe pod broken-pod' and find the failure reason in the Events section, noting it down. After the diagnosis, delete the Pod, then create a new Pod named 'fixed-pod' using the correct image ('nginx:stable') and confirm it reaches Running status.
tasks.starter:
# Create the broken Pod
kubectl run broken-pod --image=nginx:doesnotexist999

# Check status repeatedly (run 2-3 times a few seconds apart)
kubectl get pods

# Once STATUS shows ErrImagePull or ImagePullBackOff, inspect in detail
kubectl describe pod broken-pod
# Look for the 'Failed to pull image' message in the Events section

# Delete after diagnosis
kubectl delete pod broken-pod

# Recreate with the correct image
kubectl run fixed-pod --image=___
kubectl get pods
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create the broken Pod
kubectl run broken-pod --image=nginx:doesnotexist999
# Output: pod/broken-pod created (the creation request itself succeeds)

# 2. Check status (after 20-30 seconds)
kubectl get pods
# Expected output:
# NAME         READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE
# broken-pod   0/1     ImagePullBackOff   0          35s
# Note: it first appears as ErrImagePull, then transitions to ImagePullBackOff as retry intervals grow

# 3. Diagnose the cause with describe
kubectl describe pod broken-pod
# Key Events content:
#   Warning  Failed  ...  Failed to pull image "nginx:doesnotexist999":
#            rpc error: code = NotFound desc = failed to pull and unpack image
#            ...: not found
#   Warning  Failed  ...  Error: ErrImagePull
#   Normal   BackOff ...  Back-off pulling image "nginx:doesnotexist999"

# 4. Delete the broken Pod
kubectl delete pod broken-pod

# 5. Recreate with the correct image
kubectl run fixed-pod --image=nginx:stable
# Output: pod/fixed-pod created

# 6. Confirm Running status
kubectl get pods
# Expected output:
# NAME        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# fixed-pod   1/1     Running   0          12s

# 7. Clean up
kubectl delete pod fixed-pod
tasks.explanation: Even though the Pod creation request succeeds (pod created), whether the container actually runs is determined only when kubelet attempts to pull the image. A non-existent image tag causes the registry to return a 404, and kubelet records this as 'ErrImagePull'. Kubernetes retries the pull using exponential backoff, increasing the interval between attempts - this waiting state is 'ImagePullBackOff'. The Events section in 'describe' includes 'Failed to pull image' along with the specific error reason, making it the first diagnostic command to run when a Pod is stuck at 0/1. Image name typos, private registry authentication failures, and non-existent tags are the most common culprits in production.
hints: If STATUS does not change and the Pod stays at Pending for a long time, the issue may be insufficient node resources rather than an image pull failure. In that case, 'describe' Events will show an 'Insufficient cpu/memory' message instead.

ErrImagePull and ImagePullBackOff are two stages of the same problem. ErrImagePull appears immediately after the first failure; ImagePullBackOff is the state where Kubernetes is waiting before the next retry. Both indicate an image issue.
The full list of valid tags for the official nginx image is available on Docker Hub (hub.docker.com/_/nginx/tags). The most commonly used ones are 'latest', 'stable', and 'alpine'.

tasks.title: Write a Multi-Container (Sidecar) Pod YAML and Check Each Container's Logs
tasks.prompt:
Write a YAML file from scratch that defines two containers inside a single Pod.
- Main container: name 'web', image 'nginx', port 80
- Sidecar container: name 'log-watcher', image 'busybox', args that run '/bin/sh -c while true; do echo sidecar-running; sleep 5; done' as an infinite loop

Name the file 'sidecar-pod.yaml' and the Pod 'sidecar-demo'. After creating it, do the following.
1. Confirm the READY column in 'kubectl get pods' shows '2/2'.
2. Print the logs of the web container using '-c web', and the logs of the sidecar using '-c log-watcher'.
3. Verify that both containers share the same Pod IP by checking the IP field in 'kubectl describe pod sidecar-demo' and running 'kubectl exec -it sidecar-demo -c web -- printenv HOSTNAME'.
4. Delete the Pod when done.
tasks.starter:
# Write sidecar-pod.yaml (fill in the blank)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sidecar-demo
spec:
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
    - name: log-watcher
      image: busybox
      args:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - ___  # infinite loop that prints 'sidecar-running' every 5 seconds
tasks.solution:
# Completed sidecar-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sidecar-demo
spec:
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
    - name: log-watcher
      image: busybox
      args:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - while true; do echo sidecar-running; sleep 5; done

# 1. Create the Pod
kubectl apply -f sidecar-pod.yaml
# Output: pod/sidecar-demo created

# 2. Confirm READY 2/2
kubectl get pods
# Expected output:
# NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# sidecar-demo   2/2     Running   0          20s

# 3. web container logs (nginx startup message)
kubectl logs sidecar-demo -c web
# Expected output: nginx initialization logs

# 4. log-watcher container logs
kubectl logs sidecar-demo -c log-watcher
# Expected output:
# sidecar-running
# sidecar-running
# (every 5 seconds)

# 5. Check Pod IP with describe
kubectl describe pod sidecar-demo | grep -E '^IP:'
# Expected output: IP:   10.244.0.5
# -> both containers share the same IP

# 6. Check HOSTNAME env var of the web container (matches Pod name)
kubectl exec -it sidecar-demo -c web -- printenv HOSTNAME
# Expected output: sidecar-demo

# 7. Clean up
kubectl delete -f sidecar-pod.yaml
# Or: kubectl delete pod sidecar-demo
tasks.explanation: A multi-container Pod is defined by listing multiple entries in the spec.containers array. Since both containers share the same Pod IP, 'describe' shows only one IP field and the containers can communicate over localhost. Without the '-c <container-name>' flag, 'kubectl logs' will fail with 'Error: a container name must be specified for pod sidecar-demo, because it has multiple containers' - always specify the container name. The '2/2' in the READY column means both containers in the Pod are ready; if one crashes, it drops to '1/2'. The busybox infinite loop is a simplified stand-in for the pattern where a sidecar must terminate together with the main application.
hints: In the YAML args array, the entire while-loop command must be a single string element. The element that follows '-c' must be one string so that /bin/sh interprets it as a script.

If a container enters CrashLoopBackOff, it means the command exited immediately. Running just 'echo hello' in busybox without an infinite loop causes the container to exit, triggering repeated restarts. Make sure to use an infinite loop.

To actually verify localhost communication between the two containers, run 'kubectl exec -it sidecar-demo -c log-watcher -- wget -qO- localhost:80'. You will receive the nginx response from inside the log-watcher container.
